coredata.dc:title: Outstanding anti-bacterial thin-film composite membrane prepared by incorporating silver-based metal–organic framework (Ag-MOF) for water treatment

Gong, Ji-Lai
Fang, Si-Yuan
Li, Juan
Cao, Wei-Cheng
Chen, Zeng-Ping
coredata.dc:description: Biofouling was a considerable issue in the water treatment by membrane technology. In this study, high anti-bacterial property Ag-MOF was synthesized followed by embedding into polyamide layer with vacuum-assisted interfacial polymerization process to prepare an Ag-MOF incorporated membrane. The successful preparation of Ag-MOF and membranes was verified by FT-IR, FESEM, XPS, XRD, BET, zeta potential and contact angle test. On account to the strong hydrophility, porous structure, electronegativity and antibacterial property of Ag-MOF, modified membranes showed excellent performance for dyes, salts and bacteria. The water permeability of modified membranes was increased from 9.2 to 14.3 L m-2h−1 bar−1 and the rejection rate of MO, MB and RB of the optimum membranes were 95.4%, 87.8% and 99.3%, respectively. The rejection rate of Na2SO4 and MgSO4 were increased from 54.0% and 36.6% to 84.1% and 73.1%. The sterilization rate of modified membranes to E. coli and S. aureus both exceeded 99%. The leaching test of Ag+ evaluated the potential mechanism of anti-bacterial property. Moreover, dynamic bio-fouling test were used to detect the actual status in the filtration process. Those results suggested that modified membranes showed a strong resistance to bio-foulants thus enlarged the scope of application of membranes for water treatment.

head.ce:title: Outstanding anti-bacterial thin-film composite membrane prepared by incorporating silver-based metal–organic framework (Ag-MOF) for water treatment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biofouling was a considerable issue in the water treatment by membrane technology. In this study, high anti-bacterial property Ag-MOF was synthesized followed by embedding into polyamide layer with vacuum-assisted interfacial polymerization process to prepare an Ag-MOF incorporated membrane. The successful preparation of Ag-MOF and membranes was verified by FT-IR, FESEM, XPS, XRD, BET, zeta potential and contact angle test. On account to the strong hydrophility, porous structure, electronegativity and antibacterial property of Ag-MOF, modified membranes showed excellent performance for dyes, salts and bacteria. The water permeability of modified membranes was increased from 9.2 to 14.3 L m-2h−1 bar−1 and the rejection rate of MO, MB and RB of the optimum membranes were 95.4%, 87.8% and 99.3%, respectively. The rejection rate of Na2SO4 and MgSO4 were increased from 54.0% and 36.6% to 84.1% and 73.1%. The sterilization rate of modified membranes to E. coli and S. aureus both exceeded 99%. The leaching test of Ag+ evaluated the potential mechanism of anti-bacterial property. Moreover, dynamic bio-fouling test were used to detect the actual status in the filtration process. Those results suggested that modified membranes showed a strong resistance to bio-foulants thus enlarged the scope of application of membranes for water treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industry in past decades, the emission of industrial and domestic wastewater has been exploded. As an important source of industrial sewage, the textile wastewater contains a large number of organic dyes and salt ions, which severely threats the ecological environment and human health due to the recalcitrance, carcinogenicity and teratogenicity [1,2]. In addition, pathogenic bacteria in domestic sewage tend to be discharged into the surrounding aquatic environment with the effluent and then proliferate [3]. The complexity of composition in surface water makes the purification process very difficult. With the evolution of technology, membrane technology is widely used in water treatment because of the high efficiency, low cost and environmental friendliness [4]. However, membrane biofouling is a main bottleneck to hinder the further progress, which limits the application field of membranes. As the main cause of biofouling, Accumulated bacteria usually form a compact layer called biofilm, Once the biofilm fully covers the membrane surface, not only the transportation channels between water molecules and membrane are impeded, but the functional layer above the substrate membrane can be biodegraded by hydrolysis, which significantly reduces the selective separation performance [5]. Therefore, preparing a membrane with anti-bacterial property for water treatment is extremely urgent.

Various anti-bacterial membranes have been prepared by adding different materials. For instance, He et al. incorporated graphene oxide (GO) in the polyamide (PA) film to achieve approximately 80% bacteriostasis rate [6]; Weng and co-workers chose N-aminoethyl piperazine propane sulfonate (AEPPS) as the monomer to react with 1,3,5-benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC) forming a zwitterionic polyamide membranes, which significantly reduced the number of adhered bacteria [7]. Mahat’s group covered a thin layer which consisted of carbon quantum dot on polysulfone membranes, and the antibacterial performance for E. coli was improved obviously [8]. However, since some organic materials improve anti-bacterial performance indirectly by modifying the physiochemical property of membrane such as hydrophility, roughness of membrane surface and charge characteristic, the bacteria are not killed directly and tend to accumulate on membrane surface as the operation time increases. Studies have shown that some inorganic materials such as metal particles and metal oxides can directly inactivate bacteria by physical damage and reactive oxygen species [9]. Zhang et al. synthesized copper particles and the bacteriostasis rate to E. coli reached 98% [10]. Unfortunately, despite the superior antibacterial property of inorganic particles, their compatibility in organic membranes is poor, which leads to serious leakage. Therefore, the synthesis of antibacterial materials that can stably exist in the organic membrane has become the direction of research. Recent studies have shown that it is a simple and available method to modify inorganic antibacterial agent with organic materials, which can improve the stability of membrane without damaging antibacterial properties.

Silver materials have been widely studied for their excellent antibacterial property. the silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) prepared by a simple reduction reaction are the most common antibacterial agent in researches. But in practical application, the defects of pure Ag materials as membrane antibacterial agent cannot be ignored. Firstly, the external conditions in the reduction process are difficult to control, temperature, brightness and reaction time all affect the yield and quality of AgNPs [11]. A large number of expensive silver reagents can be wasted during the reaction, resulting in the high cost of the synthesis method. In addition, reductants such as borohydride, dimethylformamide and thiol can produce many by-products, which not only increase the cost of materials and subsequent treatment, but also damage ecological safety and threaten human health [12–14]. With the development of technology, some researches choose to utilize cheap materials or even biological materials to synthesize AgNPs with low cost. Dong et al. utilized tannic acid (TA) as precursors to generate AgNPs and the mortalities of E. coli and B. subtilis were almost 100% [15]. Kim and co-workers prepared AgNPs with improved yield and purity by using Laminaria japonica algal extract [16]. However, the repulsion between pure inorganic particles and organic membranes should be considered in the practical application. The poor compatibility and the inhomogeneous distribution of AgNPs in PA layers make them are easily released, which dramatically reduces the stability and antibacterial performance of membranes [17]. Furthermore, leaky AgNPs can cause secondary pollution to environment and toxicity to human health. Based on the limitation of high cost and potential toxicity, the promotion of AgNPs to industrial application is impractical. Therefore, green, efficient and low-cost synthesis methods have become the research trend. The metal–organic frameworks (MOF) in which the target metal ions can be anchored have attracted the attention of researchers. The special skeleton structure of MOF enhances the stability of silver materials, which reduces the cost of reagents and subsequent processing.

Ag-MOF is a crystalline material with stereochemical structure formed by coordination between silver ions and organic ligands. The silver materials can be fixed in the MOF material as a coordination site thus reducing the consumption of silver materials. Herein, we prepared Ag-MOF through a simple one-step hydrothermal synthesis, which reduced the amount of silver reagent compared with other traditional AgNPs preparation method. Ag-MOF combines the good compatibility of organic materials and excellent anti-bacterial property of silver materials. The stable crystal structure of Ag-MOF reduces the leakage of effective substance and improves the stability and antibacterial property without sacrificing the permeability and separation performance of modified membranes. Therefore, synthetic Ag-MOF nanoparticles with different weight concentrations are introduced into PA selective layer through the interfacial polymerization (IP) on the poly-dopamine (PDA) modified polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) substrate membrane to prepare modified thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes. The physiochemical and structural properties of modified membranes are detected by various characterization methods. The permeation, separation of dyes and salts, anti-bacterial and stability experiments are also conducted to estimate the performance of membrane in the filtration process.

PVDF substrate membrane (0.22 μm) was supplied by Shanghai Xingya purification equipment Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China. Piperazine (PIP, 99%) and 1,3,5-benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC, 98%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China. Triethylamine (TEA), tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris), silver nitrate (AgNO3) sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4) and N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China. p-Phthalic acid (PTA) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China. Rhodamine B (RB), methylene blue (MB), and congo red (CR) were provided by Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, Tianjin, China. Luria-Bertani broth (LB) was supplied by Qingdao Hope Bio-Technology Co., Ltd, Qingdao, China. Eosin-Methylene Blue Agar (EMB) was obtained from Ssincere Bio-chemical technology Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China. Mannitol salt agar (MSA) was purchased from Guangdong Huankai Microbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd, Guangzhou, China. E. coli CMCC44102 and S. aureus CMCC (B) 26,003 were purchased from Beijing Microbiological Culture Collection Center Co, Beijing, China.

The Ag-MOF synthesis method was modified by the previous study [18]. Briefly, 2 mmol AgNO3 and 2 mmol PTA were dissolved in 40 mL DMF solution, which was continuously stirred in the dark condition for 30 min to reach a uniform mixing. Subsequently, the prepared mixture was transferred into 100 mL Teflon-lined reactors and heated at 140 °C for 10 h. After reactors were cool down, the precipitates were washed twice by DMF and collected through vacuum filtration. The intercepted materials were put in an oven and dried at 60 °C for 18 h to remove the unreacted reagents and residual impurities.

The PVDF membrane was chosen as the support membrane. As shown in Figure S1, PVDF membranes were immersed in the Tris-DA solution (2 mg/mL) and kept shaking to realize the self-polymerization of dopamine. After the reaction completed, membranes were taken out and washed by deionized (DI) water. The synthetic Ag-MOFs were introduced into the polyamide layer by a vacuum filtration-assembled interfacial polymerization (IP) technique. In the IP process, PIP (2 wt%), sodium hydroxide (0.2 wt%), triethylamine (0.5 wt%) and different concentrations of Ag-MOF were added in DI water to form aqueous monomer solution, TMC was dissolved in n-hexane to form 0.1 wt% organic monomer solution. PDA-covered membranes (PDA-PVDF) firstly immersed in aqueous monomer solution for 3 min, the residual solutions were removed by vacuum pumping and membranes were air dried. Whereafter, PIP-adsorbed membranes were immersed in organic monomer solution for 30 s and excess solutions were poured off from the top of reactor. The prepared TFN membranes were washed twice with n-hexane to remove the remaining TMC and dried in oven at 60 °C to evaporate remaining n-hexane and facilitate the IP reaction. All prepared membranes were immersed into DI water overnight to reach a stable state for subsequent performance test and characterization. The prepared TFC membranes were marked as M0, M1, M2, M3 and M4 according to the mass concentration of Ag-MOF in the aqueous monomer solution is 0 wt%, 0.005 wt%, 0.01 wt%, 0.015 wt% and 0.02 wt%, respectively. Membranes with pure AgNPs (NP membrane) were prepared as traditional anti-bacterial membranes to compare their performance to M2 and evaluate the superiority of synthetic Ag-MOF. The detailed procedures are exhibited in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Ag-MOF and modified membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Ag-MOF and prepared membranes were measured by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) with scanning range of 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1 (Bruker ALPHA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was obtained using Bruker D8 advance powder X-ray Cu Kα radiation diffractometer in 2theta from 5° to 90° at a scanning rate of 5°/min. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) was used to captured the surface and cross-section morphology of materials (Zeiss Sigma 300). To further confirm the elemental composition and chemical bonds of Ag-MOF and prepared membranes, X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) was detected by Thermo fisher Scientific K-Alpha. Brunner-Emmet-Teller (BET) measurement used nitrogen adsorption method to evaluate the pore size of membranes (Micromeritics ASAP 2460). Water contact angle was tested by the equipment of JY-82B Kruss DSA (German) to evaluate the hydrophilicity of membranes. Zeta potentials of membranes were conducted by Surpass3 solid surface zeta potential analyzer with the use of 1 mmol KCl solution (pH = 7.0 ± 0.2) at 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Permeability and separation performance of membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The evaluations of transport and separation property were tested in a home-made laboratory dead-end filtration equipment with an effective area surface of 12.56 cm2 at room temperature. All modified TFC membranes were pre-pressurized with DI water at 2 bars for 30 min to reach membranes stability. After the pre-filtration process, permeability and rejection rate involving dyes (i.e., MO, MB, RB) and salts (i.e., Na2SO4, MgSO4, NaCl, MgCl2) were measured. The initial concentration of feed solution was set as 10 mg/L. The permeability (JF, L·m2 h−1 bar−1) was observed with the weight change of solution in the same interval of 30 min at 2 bars. The computational formula of permeability was shown in equation (1) below:
J F = Δ m / ( A × Δ t × P )
where Δm (kg) means the weight variation before and after the permeation process, A (m2) represents the effective area surface of membrane (12.56 cm2), Δt (h) is the time interval of permeation process (0.5 h) and P (bar) is the eternal pressure (2 bars).

Rejection rate was calculated by the concentration of feed liquid and filtrate as equation (2):
R
( % )
= [
(
C f
-
C p
)
/
C f
] × 100
where Cf and Cp represent the concentration of dyes and salts (mg/L) in the feed liquid and filtrate, respectively. The dye concentration was measured by ultraviolet spectrophotometer. The salt concentration was conducted by conductive meter (Mettler Toledo SevenEasy S30). All results were obtained by three independent experiments.

To analyze the molecular weight cut off (MWCO) of membrane, polyethylene glycol (PEG) with different molecular weight (300, 400, 600, 800, 1000 Da) was chosen as the feed solution. The filtration process of PEG was conducted under the same operational condition of above separation experiment. The concentration of PEG was measured by total organic carbon analyzer (TOC-VCPH Shimadze, Japan) and the rejection rate of PEG solution was calculated by equation (2). MWCO value of membrane was defined as the molecular weight when the rejection rate reached to 90% [19].

The anti-bacterial property of modified membranes was measured by plate count method and inhibition zone experiments. The E. coli and S. aureus bacteria were determined as Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria representative strain, respectively. Firstly, both bacteria were inoculated in the sterilized LB broth at 37 °C with 180 rpm shaking overnight. The obtained bacterial suspension was injected into a fresh sterilized LB broth at a volume ratio of 1:100 and was incubated at 37 °C for 3 h with shaking. After this incubation process, the bacterial suspension was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 3 min and the liquid supernatant was poured off. The bacterial sediments were washed and resuspended with the sterilized phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.4). The obtained bacterial suspension was gradually diluted and finally, a bacterial suspension with concentration of 107 colony form unit (CFU)/mL was prepared. Modified membrane samples were cut into a surface aera of 1 cm2 tablet and immersed into Eppendorf (ep) tubes which contain 1 mL above prepared bacterial suspension. The mixture was incubated at 37 °C with mild shaking for 1 h. After this period, membrane slices were taken out and washed twice by PBS to rinse the un-adsorbed bacteria. The cleaned membranes were put into sterilized centrifuge tubes containing 10 mL PBS and sonicated for 10 min to form the final suspension. The resulting E. coli and S. aureus suspensions with 100 μL were adsorbed and coated on the EMB agar plate and MSA plate, respectively. the EMB agars and MSA plates were incubated statically in bio-chemical incubator at 37 °C for 24 h and the CFU was counted.

The inhibition zone experiments were carried out in the similar way. Briefly, the fresh sterilized EMB agar and MSA agar plates were prepare, which were subsequently coated with 100 μL the above prepared 107 CFU/mL bacterial suspension. The control membrane and modified membranes were cut into disks with the diameter of 6 mm and the effective side of membranes faced down in the center of agar plates then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h and the inhibition zone was observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Silver ions leaching test of membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The leakage of silver ions was evaluated by a simple leaching test. Briefly, modified membrane scraps (2 cm2) were immersed into 20 mL DI water and shook at 120 rpm for 7 days. The solution samples were replaced everyday with fresh DI water, all obtained water samples were filtrated then acidified by HNO3 for subsequent detection. The concentration of Ag+ was measured by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, Agilent 7900, USA)

The biofouling activity of prepared membranes was assessed by a dynamic water/bacteria cycle experiment. A synthetic water sample was prepared followed by other literatures [17,18,20]: 0.5 mM CaCl2, 0.6 mM MgSO4, 0.5 mM NaHCO3, 2.5 mM NaCl, 0.45 mM KH2PO4, 0.9 mM NH4Cl. Above prepared bacterial suspension was poured into synthetic wastewater to form a bacterial solution with the bacterial concentration of 107 CFU/L. Prepared membranes were firstly filtrated with DI water by the same operating conditions as permeability experiment mentioned above then biological wastewater was permeated at the same operation. After finishing the filtration process of bacterial solution, the equipment and membrane samples were flushed by water and the overall system was filtrated with DI water again. The water flux of the whole cycle process was recorded at same 10 min interval, the acquired data were used to calculate the flux recovery rate (FRR) and the total fouling rate (Rt), the formulas were shown followed:
F R R
( % )
=
(
J 2
/
J 1
)
× 100
R t
( % )
= [
(
J 1
-
J f
)
/
J 1
] × 100

J1 means the water flux of membranes every 10 min in the first stage, Jf means the permeability of both bacterial solutions, while J2 represents the water flux of membranes after the washing operation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of synthetic Ag-MOF and prepared membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical groups of Ag-MOF and prepared membranes could be analyzed by FT-IR and the obtained results were presented in Fig. 1 . As the organic ligand of Ag-MOF, numerous characteristic peaks could be certified as chemical bond of terephthalic acid. (Fig. 1a) depicts the pattern of Ag-MOF. The peaks in the range of 800 to 1250 cm−1 were normally assigned to the C-H bending vibration in benzene ring. The lower peaks such as 819 cm−1 and 885 cm−1 were attributed to out-plane C-H bending vibration while the peaks at high wave number such as 1012 cm−1 and 1085 cm−1 were attributed to in-plane C-H bending vibration [21–23]. The peak at 1288 cm−1 was associated with the combination of O-H bending vibration and C-O asymmetric stretching vibration in carboxylic acid group [24,25]. The peak at 1311 cm−1 was corresponding to C-O bending vibration in the Ag-MOF. The O-C-O stretching vibration in Ag-MOF and the symmetric stretching vibration of the linkage between C-O and silver were reflected at 1382 cm−1 [26,27]. The peak at 1438 cm−1 was consistent with the C = O symmetric stretching vibration and O-H bending vibration [18,28,29]. The obvious peak at 1568 cm−1 was identified as C = C stretching vibration in the benzene ring skeleton and the asymmetric stretching vibration of the linkage between C-O and silver [23,25,30]. The peaks at 503 cm−1, 451 cm−1 and 744 cm−1 were assigned to the Ag-O stretching vibration [31,32], which proved the successful coordination between AgNO3 and terephthalic acid.

In order to analyze the effect of Ag-MOF concentration on the functional groups of prepared polyamide membranes, pristine membranes and modified membranes with different Ag-MOF concentration were also detected by FT-IR and the spectra were exhibited in (Fig. 1b). The broad and moderate peaks around 3445 cm−1 and the significant peaks at 1619 cm−1 were both found in pristine and modified membranes, which were attributed to O-H stretching vibration of carboxylic group and C = O stretching vibration of amide group in semi-aromatic PA membranes, indicating the successful polymerization reaction between PIP and TMC [33,34]. Since unreacted acyl chloride groups could be hydrolyzed into carboxylic groups in IP process, obvious characteristics are both observed at 1442 cm−1 and 1249 cm−1 in pristine and modified membranes, which were assigned to symmetric and stretching vibration of C = O and asymmetric stretching vibration of C-O in carboxyl groups, respectively[18,24]. However, because Ag-MOF were rich in free carboxylic group, the peak intensity at 1442 cm−1 and 1249 cm−1 of modified membranes was stronger than pristine membranes and positively correlated with the concentration of Ag-MOF, demonstrating the successful loading of Ag-MOF in PA film of modified membranes. The stronger peaks at 734 cm−1 and 644 cm−1 of modified membranes which were not detected in pristine membranes, were consistent with Ag-O stretching vibration [27], which directly testified the existence of silver element. The increase of silver characteristic peak intensity also indicated the improvement of Ag-MOF concentration in modified membranes.

To examine the crystalline and stereochemical structure of Ag-MOF, XRD evaluation was performed and the pattern was shown in (Fig. 2 a). Four obvious diffraction peaks could be seen at the 2theta of 38.13°, 44.32°, 66.46° and 77.39°, which were similar to the silver standard PDF card JCPS No. 04–0783 and JCPS No. 89–3722 [35,36]. The four characteristic peaks could be certified as the crystal plane (111), (200), (220) and (311) of the face-center-cubic (fcc) crystal structure [27]. In addition, the moderate peaks at 2 theta of 40.34° and 42.59° were in concordance with standard silver terephthalate card (JCPS No. 35–1679) [26]. Terminal effect and bridge pattern were the main coordination mode between Ag atom and terephthalic acid [37]. The detection statistics were corresponded to the recorded spectrum of Ag-MOF in previous literatures [23,25,38], indicating the Ag-MOF were successfully synthesized and presented regular structure. Furthermore, pristine and modified membranes were measured at the same time and results were exhibited in (Fig. 2b). A broad peak at 20° to 30° was detected in all membranes, which could be attributed to the generated PA layer [39]. Four characteristic peaks of Ag-MOF in (Fig. 2a) were observed in modified membrane but not in pristine membrane, indicating Ag-MOF were successfully introduced into PA layer and did not occur structural vibration in the IP process. The intensity of characteristic peaks of modified membrane was also increased with the improvement of Ag-MOF content.

The morphology of Ag-MOF was analyzed by FESEM and the pictures were shown in Figure S2. As illustrated in the images, Ag-MOF displayed an irregular glomerate octahedral structure and numerous Ag-MOF with different size adhere to each other to form molecular clusters, implying Ag-MOF has a tendency to aggregate in high concentration (Figure S2a). (Figure S2b) exhibited the high-resolution image of independent Ag-MOF particles. The intact three-dimensional structure of individual Ag-MOF particle was observed. However, the octahedral morphology was not completely symmetric and the particle size of Ag-MOF was approximately 439 nm. In addition, some tiny and bright spots were found to be adsorbed on the surface of Ag-MOF, which were identified as scattered Ag nanoparticles originated from the reduction reaction of AgNO3 by DMF.

The accurate information about the elemental composition and chemical bonds of Ag-MOF are recorded by XPS spectrum. The whole spectrum of Ag-MOF was shown in (Fig. 3 a) and the fitting maps of carbon, oxygen and silver were exhibited in Fig. 3 (b-d), which were presented on the peaks at 284.8 eV (C 1 s), 531.2 eV (O 1 s) and 368.6 eV (Ag 3d), respectively. For the high resolution of C 1 s spectrum, three specific peaks could be observed: an apparent peak at 284.8 eV was assigned to the C-C, C-H and C = C bond in the benzene ring; the peak of 286.5 eV was corresponding to the C-O and C-O-C bond; a moderate characteristic peak at 288.2 eV is attributed to the O-C-O and C = O group [22,40]. Regard to O 1 s, the peak located at 531.2 eV was ascribed to the Ag-O bond between silver and carboxyl group; C = O and O-C = O bond in the organic ligands appeared at 532.4 eV [24,28]. The binding energy of 368.7 eV and 374.7 eV in the Ag 3d spectrum were associated with Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively. [41]. To affirm the Ag-MOF are successfully introduced into the modified membranes, M0 and M2 were examined by XPS and the results were shown in (Fig. 3e-g). The patterns of both membranes were related to the characteristic peak of C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, which were located at 285 eV, 400 eV and 531 eV, respectively. A weak characteristic peak at 368 eV involved to Ag 3d orbit was examined in M2 and two fitting peaks were furtherly obtained at 368.0 eV and 374.0 eV, which means Ag-MOF were successfully introduced and steadily anchored into composite layer (Fig. 3e). The high-resolution spectrums of N 1 s in M0 and M2 showed similar fitting peak around 399 eV and was assigned to C-N and N-C = O bond of amide group in PA layer, indicating prepared PA layer exhibit a complete semi-aromatic structure (Fig. 3f and 3 g)

The surface and cross-section morphology of the pre-treated PDA membranes were shown in Figure S3 and prepared membranes were exhibited in Fig. 4 . As shown in (Figure S3a), a thin dense layer was observed to cover on the PVDF substrate, implying the successful self-polymerization reaction of PDA. The cross-section morphology of PDA-modified membranes showed a smooth and flat surface and no obvious layering phenomenon on the top of membrane surface, which indicated the PDA molecules were uniformly distributed and firmly anchored rather than aggregating on the top of membrane surface (Figure S3b). The adherent PDA interlayers can reduce the internal concentration polarization in the IP process thus improve the hydrophility and reduce the thickness and roughness of PA membrane [42]. The even PDA layer was favorable to subsequent IP process because monomers could equally distribute on the surface rather than penetrating into pores of substrates to avoid the blockage of the membrane [43] In addition, a strong covalent linkage could be formed between PA layers and PDA interlayers by the reaction of the quinone groups in the PDA and aqueous amine monomer [44]. The surface and cross-section images of Ag-MOF doped PA membranes were shown in Fig. 4, the surface of M0 displayed a traditional grainy and convex structures, which was corresponding to the typical structure of PA membranes (Fig. 4a). Further, the peak-and-valley structure in cross-section morphology of M0 was coincided with other literatures [17,18]. With the improvement of Ag-MOF content in PA layer, some changes in the morphology of membranes were occurred. Because Ag-MOF could connect with PIP or TMC molecules through chemical bonds, the reaction rate between monomers was declined and a moderate nodular surface was mediated [19]. Thus, the nodular structure of M1 was controlled and the surface became relative smoother and more even than M0 (Fig. 4b and 4 g). However, as the Ag-MOF mass concentration increased further from 0.01 wt% to 0.02 wt%, the morphology of membrane showed a contrasting tendency. Detailly, the nodulation of membrane surface was significantly enhanced. Although the Ag-MOF-mediated surface morphology regulation was further strengthened with the concentration improved, the severe aggregation of Ag-MOF in PA layer was the primary barrier to form a defect-free selective film. Ag-MOF with high concentration deposited on PA layer, which were appeared as large agglomerate on the surface. The specific thickness of M0 and M2 was also measured by FESEM and the patterns were presented in Figure S4. The overlapping PA layer of M0 was uniform plane and the thickness was approximately 234.5 nm (Figure S4a). After incorporated with Ag-MOF (Figure S4b), the thickness was slightly improved to 285.3 nm as Ag-MOF could mediated the IP process and produced a relative loose PA layer structure. The surface aera, pore volume and pore size of membranes were evaluated by BET and data were summarized in Table S1. The pore size of commercial PVDF membranes was 0.22 μm, after the self-polymerization of dopamine on the substrate membranes, the surface pore size of PDA-covered membranes (PDA-PVDF) decreased to 6.74 nm due to the smooth and flat dopamine interlayer [42]. With the successful IP reaction on the PDA-PVDF membrane surface, the surface aera and pore volume of PA layer both increased due to the peak-and-valley-structure and numerous nanopores [17,45]. The pore size of M0 was decreased to 3.77 nm. Since the incorporated Ag-MOF could decline the reaction rate between PIP and TMC, the formed PA layer became smoother and the pore size of M2 was decreased to 1.94 nm. The agglomeration of Ag-MOF in high concentration resulted a defective PA layer thus the pore size of M4 increased to 2.68 nm. The above morphological characteristics could impact the permeability and separation performance of membranes in the subsequent filtration test.

The elemental constitution of M0 and M2 was shown in Table S2. In theory, the carbon nitrogen ratio (C/N) represents the crosslinking degree of composite membranes. With the increase of C/N value, the crosslinking degree of selective layer decreases gradually [46]. High crosslinking degree means synthetic PA layers appear compact structure, which reveals excellent selective and separating property, while membranes with lower crosslinking degree normally assume better hydrophility and higher water permeability due to the loose PA layer [18,47]. Compared to M0, silver element occurred and the atomic percentage of oxygen improved obviously in M2, the C/N ratio enhanced from 6.0 to 6.6, suggesting the crosslinking degree of M2 reduced with the incorporation of Ag-MOF. This phenomenon was caused by that Ag-MOF was abundant in free carboxylic group, which endowed the Ag-MOF strong hydrophilicity and electronegativity. Ag-MOF could be surrounded by water molecules in aqueous solution, preventing PIP molecules from contacting with hydrophobic TMC thus the stability of formed PA layers declined and polyamide macromolecules were hard to firmly adhere to thin film and Ag-MOF. Because of the ionization of carboxyl group, the hydrophilic and electronegative Ag-MOF readily bound with the charged-positive PIP through interaction force such as hydrogen bond and electrostatic attraction, which restrained the diffusion rate and reacting dosage of PIP in the reaction interface of organic/inorganic. The IP process was severely retarded and the structure of prepared PA layer was directed altered [46,48]. In addition, the carboxylic group of Ag-MOF could link to the secondary amine in PIP to generate new amide bonds or react with acyl chloride group in TMC to form anhydride group, which hindered the formation of PA layer [19]. Furthermore, due to the generation of hydrochloric acid (HCl) in the reaction, the hydrogen ions (H+) originated from the dissociation of carboxylic groups could restrain the IP process between PIP and TMC [49]. Based on the above discussion, the introduction of Ag-MOF decreased the crosslinking degree of modified membranes by potential intermolecular force and chemical bonds thus the microstructure of functional layers was mediated gradually. The looser structure of prepared composite selective layer could effectively adjust the permeability of membrane and alleviated the transfer resistance of water molecules thus might performer higher water flux [50].

The hydrophilicity and surface charge of membranes are essential indicators which can significantly affect the permeability and antifouling property of membranes. Membranes with higher water contact angle (WCA) means strong hydrophobicity[19]. As shown in (Figure S5a), with the increase of Ag-MOF mass concentration from 0 wt% to 0.02 wt%, the WCA of membranes sharply decreased. Compared to the M0, the contact angle of M2 and M4 decreased from 44.0° to 33.7° and 25.4°, meaning the introduction of Ag-MOF significantly improved the hydrophilicity of modified membranes. This result could be rationalized by two reasons: I) hydrophilic carboxylic groups in Ag-MOF adsorbed and linked surrounding water molecules by strong hydrogen bonds and form a dense hydration layer, which acted as the molecular channels to transform water molecules thus improved the wettability of membrane surface [50–52]; II) the introduction of Ag-MOF increased the effective surface area in the reaction process and the microstructure of surface was changed, reducing the interface energy of membrane surface. As a result, the hydrophilicity of modified membranes was obviously reduced [53,54]. In general, membranes with negative charged show a repulsion force for bacterial cell thus strengthen the bio-fouling resistance [17]. As shown in (Figure S5b), all membrane samples exhibited negative charge due to the formation of electronegative polyamide layer [39]. The zeta potential of membranes enhanced from −25.9 mV of M0 to −48.3 mV of M4 with the improvement of Ag-MOF concentration, this phenomenon could be attributed to the carboxylic groups, which generated from the introduction of Ag-MOF and hydrolysis of unreacted acyl chloride groups [17,46]. The deprotonation process of residual carboxylic groups enhanced the electronegativity property of membranes [18].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Permeability and separation performance of modified membranes
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in (Fig. 5 a), the pure water permeability (PWP) of pristine membranes (M0) was only 9.2 ± 0.1 L m-2h−1 bar−1. With the incorporation of Ag-MOF, the PWP was increased synchronously and displayed a positively correlation with the Ag-MOF concentration. Compared to M0, the PWP of M2 reached to 14.3 ± 0.5 L m-2h−1 bar−1. As described above, introduced Ag-MOF effectively decreased the cross-linking degree of prepared membranes thus the generated PA layer presented a loose structure that water molecules were readily pass through the large holes in the PA layer [19]. In addition, due to the formation of a dense hydration layer, the hydrophilicity and wettability of modified membranes were improved, which was beneficial to the transport of water. Furthermore, porous Ag-MOF might directly form water channels within PA layer, water molecules were easily flowed through due to the low solution resistance and energy consumption [47,55]. Compared to pure inorganic nanomaterials, although the compatibility between Ag-MOF and PA polymer was enhanced with the organic skeleton in organic ligand, the defect void might still be generated in the IP process especially in high concentration. The generated large void in polymeric chain due to the aggregation and accumulation of Ag-MOF was enlarged with the increasement of Ag-MOF concentration, those interstices signally improved the volume fraction of void in PA layers and provide a transport pathway for water quickly penetrating [17].

The separation performance of modified membranes was also demonstrated in Fig. 5. As shown in (Fig. 5b), the permeability of all dye solutions exhibited a obvious downward trend compared with the flux of pure water due to the blockage of membrane [56]. The flux of M0 to MO (6.4 ± 0.4 L m-1h−1 bar−1) and MB (6.0 ± 0.1 L m-1h−1 bar−1) was similar and the flux of RB was slightly higher with the performance of 7.7 ± 0.2 L m-1h−1 bar−1. As the increasement of Ag-MOF, the permeation flux of three dye solutions improved markedly and the relevant permeability sequence of diverse dyes for M2 was RB, MO and MB, which reached to 11.1 ± 0.2, 9.9 ± 0.4 and 9.0 ± 0.4 L m-1h−1 bar−1, respectively. The rejection rate of MO, MB and RB of M0 was 88.1 ± 0.2%, 85.5 ± 0.5% and 92.8 ± 0.4%, respectively. With the improvement of Ag-MOF concentration, the rejection rate of MO and RB showed a trend of first increasing then decreasing and the highest rejection rate of MO and RB occurred at the mass concentration of 0.01 wt% (M2), which reached to 95.4 ± 0.4% and 99.3 ± 0.4%, respectively. For MB molecules, the rejection rate was basically stable and lower than MO and RB, which was approximately 85%. As Ag-MOF concentration increasing to 0.02 wt%, the rejection rate showed a mild drop and descended from 87.8 ± 0.3% to 82.0 ± 0.5%. The salts rejection rate of modified membranes with different Ag-MOF concentrations was shown in (Fig. 5c). The pristine membrane (M0) showed a low salts rejection rate, the rejection rates of the other three salts were all lower than 40% except Na2SO4. With the introduction of Ag-MOF, modified membranes showed a significant improvement for Na2SO4 and MgSO4, which reached to 84.1 ± 1.1% and 73.1 ± 1.5% (M2), respectively. However, the retention performance of Na2SO4 and MgSO4 did not keep improving with Ag-MOF concentration increased from 0.01 wt% to 0.02 wt% and the rejection rates of M4 (0.02 wt%) were only 82.3 ± 1.7% and 69.4 ± 0.6%. In addition, the rejection rate of monovalent anionic chloride salt did not improve obviously with the increase of Ag-MOF concentration and remained below 40%.

The interception mechanism of membranes was mainly divided into steric hinderance effect and electrostatic interaction, dye molecules and salt ions with different physiochemical property could be rejected by size sieving and electrostatic force [57]. Compared to MO and MB, the permeability and rejection rate of RB was highest among pristine and modified membranes, the explanation of this result was that RB molecules contained plentiful hydrophilic carboxylic group to reduce the solvent resistance of pollutants solutions in the filtration process thus more water molecules could favorably pass through the modified membrane [58]. Based on the fitting curve of PEG rejection rate (Figure S6), the MWCO of M2 was 401 Da, which was consistent to the results of separation experiments. The molecular weight and diameter of RB are 479.01 g/mol and 13.857 Å, which are larger than MO (327.33 g/mol and 11.841 Å) and MB (319.85 g/mol and 12.263 Å), indicating the RB molecules were readily trapped by the dense and compact membranes thus showed over 99% rejection rate [40,57]. Regard to the charged substances, the account for the difference of rejection rate was a mutual effect of sieving exclusion and electrostatic interaction [59]. As discussed earlier, Ag-MOF was electronegative thus the electronegativity of modified membranes was gradually enhanced with the increasement of Ag-MOF dosage. According to Donnan effects, divalent anionic sulfate (Na2SO4 and MgSO4) showed high rejection rate due to their strong electronegativity [60]. MO and divalent anionic sulfate (Na2SO4 and MgSO4) showed high rejection rate due to their strong electronegativity. In addition, the hydration diameter (dh) of salt ions also affected separation performance by steric hinderance effect. The dh of Cl- (6.6 Å) is smaller than SO4 2- (7.6 Å), indicating chloride salts were more likely to penetrate through the PA layer [2]. Considering the small hydration size and weak repulsion force, modified membrane showed poor retention performance for chloride salts such as NaCl and MgCl2. On the contrary, the electrostatic attraction mediated by opposite charged substance resulted in the relatively low rejection rate of MB. MB with small molecular weight were absorbed to the surface of membranes and might enter into micropore in selective layers, which was consistent to the phenomenon that the membrane flux was minimum while MB solution was filtered [40,61]. As the Ag-MOF mass concentration increased to 0.02 wt%, the rejection rate of dyes and salts did not continuously increase or even partly declined, which was because the aggregation of Ag-MOF could not be ignored. The rough and inhomogeneity surface provided more binding and anchoring sites for pollutants. The spontaneous aggregation between Ag-MOF might produce some space structures that damaged the integrity of PA membranes and the size was sufficient to make both dye molecules and salt ions to pass through [57,62].

The performance evaluation of NP membranes was conducted and results were compared to the optimal membrane (M2). As shown in (Figure S7a), the PWP of NP membranes was similar to the M2, which were both approximately 14 L m-2h−1 bar−1, but the flux and dyes rejection rate of NP membranes were declined obviously. Likewise, NP membranes showed a lower rejection rate of divalent salts compared with M2 (Figure S7b). Table S3 listed the zeta potential and water contact angle of both membranes. Due to the oxidation process of AgNPs on the membrane surface, a silver oxide layer may be formed thus exhibit negative charge [41,63]. In addition, AgNPs can effectively enhances the wettability of membrane surface [13]. However, as AgNPs lack the hydrophilic and negative charged carboxylic groups compared with Ag-MOF, the hydrophilicity and electronegativity of NP membranes were lower than M2, resulting in the lower flux and rejection rate of NP membrane for dyes and salts. Furthermore, the incompatibility between inorganic AgNPs and polyamide leads to the formation of a defective PA layer containing aggregates and gaps (Figure S8a, b), which also explains the reduced rejection rate of dyes and salts in NP membranes [63].

The experimental result was shown in Fig. 6 . The pristine membrane (M0) had almost no bacteriostatic effect on both types of bacteria. With the addition of Ag-MOF, the number of bacterial colonies growing on the exogenous culture medium was significantly reduced, suggesting the sterilization effect was dramatically improved even at the low concentration. The antibacterial rate to E. coli and S. aureus of M1 was increased to 78% and 82%, respectively. As the Ag-MOF concentration reached to 0.02 wt%, no obvious bacterial colony was observed and the antibacterial rate of two bacteria both exceeded to 99%, indicating the sterilization effect induced by Ag-MOF was gradually strengthened with the increasement of the dosage. The antibacterial experiment results were shown in (Figure S7c), the sterilization rate of NP membranes and M2 both exceeded 90%. The consequence obtained from inhibition zone experiments was consistent with hereinabove (Fig. 6b). The bacteria grown on the plate medium that covered membrane samples (M0) to form bacterial communities. Upon decorated by Ag-MOF, a distinct circular aera that contained no bacterial colony and the diameter was used to evaluate the antibacterial activity of samples. The diameter of inhibition zone for E. coli and S. aureus in M1 was 0.9 cm and 1.2 cm, respectively, and the value was lengthened to 2.0 cm and 2.6 cm with the Ag-MOF increased to 0.02 wt%, which proved the outstanding antibacterial property.

To confirm the effective sterilizing materials and potential mechanism of anti-biofouling performance, the Ag+ leaching test was conducted and the result was shown in Figure S9. The leakage of Ag+ in M2 was 2.8 ± 0.5 μg/L on the first day and showed a descending trend with time proceeding. On the sixth days, the leaking amount of Ag+ was stable and only 1.0 ± 0.4 μg/L. the lower leakage of Ag+ not only improved the anti-bacterial property of modified membranes, but reduced the risk of secondary contamination. Bacterial death was a complex activity mediated by a series of physiological changes and internal metabolic process. The potential mechanism of the preeminent antibacterial property of Ag-MOF could be divided into two categories: the damage of bacterial integrity and the internal inactivation. I) Ag-MOF was a reservoir for Ag+ and the Ag+ could be leaked from the framework structure [17,24]. The Ag+ acted as cationic agent to contact cytoderm and cytomembrane of bacteria, which damaged ion channels and destroyed the external structure. The destruction of bacterial integrity led to severe leakage of intercellular materials and eventually cause an irreversible bacteria disintegration and death [18,20,64,65]. II) Reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated by Ag-MOF could react with intercellular substances that undertaken essential metabolic activities. The important infrastructure of bacterial or basic biomacromolecules were oxidized and the original intracellular substances changed their morphological structure or chemical composition thus lose the primary function, triggering a large-scale disruption of metabolism and an obvious dysfunction [17,41,64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic biofouling evaluation of modified membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the impact of Ag-MOF on the biofouling performance in the filtration process, dynamic biofouling experiment was conducted in the synthetic sewage containing E. coli and S. aureus with 107 CFU/L concentration. (Fig. 7 a) illustrated the water flux reduction of M0 and M2 for both bacterial wastewaters. Results showed that pristine and modified membranes both exhibited a downtrend for bacterial wastewater. Compared to M0, the decrease in flux of M2 was more moderate, which could be suggested the inhibiting effect of biofilm formation induced from Ag-MOF Moreover, the flux recovery rate (FRR) and the total fouling ratio (Rt) in the whole cycle process of M0 and M2 were shown in (Fig. 7b). The bio-fouling of M0 was more serious than M2, the FRR of M0 was only 53.5% and 53.4%, and the Rt of M0 was as high as 63.6% and 65.8% for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. This phenomenon revealed that pristine membranes were easily contaminated by bio-foulants and those adsorbed foulants were difficult to be removed due to the formation of biofilm. However, the FRR of M2 reached to 91.4% and 91.2% for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively, suggesting most bacteria could not adhere firmly on the membrane surface and were easily flushed from PA layer by water in the water/bacterial solution circle. The Rt of M2 to E. coli and S. aureus was only 25.0% and 26.3%, indicating the excellent antibacterial performance of modified membranes. Those results were attributed to the improvement of hydrophilicity and electronegativity, causing a strong repulsion force to prevent the adsorption and deposition of bacteria. Furthermore, the adsorptive bacteria tended to gather on the surface of membranes and form a composite biofilm, the extracellular polymeric substances increased the transport friction to water molecules and the permeability process was limited. The Ag-MOF with autologous antibacterial property directly killed bacteria, restraining the formation and extension of biofilm [17,24,38,65]. Therefore, the modified membranes could effectively relieve the fouling pressure and reduce the maintenance costs in the operating process.

In this research, Ag-MOF doped-membrane was successfully prepared by vacuum-assemble IP method. Modified membranes showed excellent hydrophilicity, strong electronegativity, homogeneous distribution and outstanding antibacterial property. Based on the above characteristic, modified membranes exhibited satisfactory performance. The PWP increased from 9.2 ± 0.1 to 14.3 ± 0.5 L m-2h−1 bar−1 with the Ag-MOF mass concentration increased from 0 wt% to 0.01 wt% and the rejection rate of divalent anionic sulfate (Na2SO4 and MgSO4) increased from 52.8 ± 1.6% and 36.6 ± 1.4% to 84.1 ± 1.1% and 73.1 ± 1.5%. For different dyes, the rejection efficiency of optimal concentration (0.01 wt%) was above 85% and that of MO and RB exceeded 95%. The improvement of separation rate was attributed to size sieving and electrostatic interaction effect. In addition, results of anti-bacterial experiments proved that modified membrane showed high anti-bacterial property and sterilizing rate of modified membranes almost reached to 100% for both bacteria. Furthermore, the leaching test demonstrated that Ag+ leached from membrane acted as effective sterilizing materials for bio-foulants, the minor leakage of silver materials from modified membranes (1.0 ± 0.4 μg/L) showed the lower toxicity and higher stability. The FRR of E. coli and S. aureus in optimal membrane both exceeded 90% in the dynamic biofouling evaluation, indicating adhered bacteria on modified membrane surface could be removed by water flushing operation. The potential mechanism of Ag-MOF antibacterial activities was divided into the damage of bacterial integrity and the internal inactivation. These results confirm that the synthesized Ag-MOF is stable in the PA layer, which reduces the cost of traditional silver materials due to the regeneration and ecotoxicity.

In summary, the Ag-MOF-dopped membranes synthesized in this work show the characteristics of convenient preparation, low cost and environmental friendliness. Compared with other antibacterial silver materials reported in literatures, Ag-MOF uses organic ligand in the material structure to compensate for the defects of AgNPs. In addition, the incorporation of Ag-MOF not only endows modified membrane with excellent antibacterial property, but effectively improves the permeability, separation performance and stability of modified membranes. Furthermore, the dynamic biofouling experiments verifies that the modified membrane can remove the adsorbed bacteria by flushing in actual operation, which relieves the biofouling pressure in the membrane operation. The simple and efficient modification method provides a promising candidate to prepare antibacterial membrane for water treatment.

Zi-Kang Tan: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ji-Lai Gong: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Si-Yuan Fang: Resources, Investigation. Juan Li: Resources. Wei-Cheng Cao: Resources. Zeng-Ping Chen: Conceptualization.
